blocks.text: As it is written in the prophet Isaiah, “See, I am sending my messenger ahead of you, who will prepare your way;
the voice of one crying out in the wilderness: ‘Prepare the way of the Lord, make his paths straight,’”

2–3 : Mark combines Isa 40.3 with elements of Ex 23.20 and Mal 3.1. , or “deserted place,” an important theme in Mark (1.4,12,13,35,45; 6.31,32,35) and the biblical setting of Israel's deliverance (Ex 6; 13.17–22; Isa 41.18–20; 43.19–21; Hos 2.14–15).
John the baptizer appeared in the wilderness, proclaiming a baptism of repentance for the forgiveness of sins.
4 : , a ritual cleansing performed, in this case, apart from institutional religious sanction and preparing for God's renewal of the earth. See Isa 1.16–17.
And people from the whole Judean countryside and all the people of Jerusalem were going out to him, and were baptized by him in the river Jordan, confessing their sins.
Now John was clothed with camel's hair, with a leather belt around his waist, and he ate locusts and wild honey.
6 : John's clothing resembles the prophet Elijah's (2 Kings 1.8; cf. Mal 4.5–6); cf. 6.15; 8.28; 15.35–36.
He proclaimed, “The one who is more powerful than I is coming after me; I am not worthy to stoop down and untie the thong of his sandals.
7 : , possible allusion to a messianic figure (see Isa 11.2; . . 17.37; cf. Mk 3.27).
I have baptized you with water; but he will baptize you witha the Holy Spirit.”
1.2–8: John appears in the wilderness (Mt 3.1–12; Lk 3.1–20; Jn 1.19–28).
In those days Jesus came from Nazareth of Galilee and was baptized by John in the Jordan.
9 : , a small village in southern , the base for Jesus's public ministry (1.14,16; 39; 3.7; 7.31; 9.30; etc.).
And just as he was coming up out of the water, he saw the heavens torn apart and the Spirit descending like a dove on him.
10 : , the rending of the heavens accompanies the divine penetration of the created realm (Isa 64.1; Ezek 1.1; see also Mk 15.38), through (see Joel 2.28–32; Acts 2.17–22).
And a voice came from heaven, “You are my Son, the Beloved; with you I am well pleased.”
1.9–11: John baptizes Jesus (Mt 3.13–17; Lk 3.21–22; Jn 1.29–34).
11 : , combines Ps 2.7 with Isa 42.1 to assign royal status to God's servant. Cf. 1.1; 9.7; 14.61; 15.39.
And the Spirit immediately drove him out into the wilderness.
12 : , a typically Markan term that lends urgency to the story.
He was in the wilderness forty days, tempted by Satan; and he was with the wild beasts; and the angels waited on him.
1.12–13: Wilderness testing (cf. Mt 4.1–11; Lk 4.1–13). Mark does not elaborate on the nature of Jesus's testing.
13 : , a powerful evil figure who opposes power associated with God's coming kingdom (see 3.23–27; 4.15; 8.33).
Now after John was arrested, Jesus came to Galilee, proclaiming the good news of God,
14 : , the message that “the kingdom of God has come near” (v. 15) in the life and death of Jesus.
and saying, “The time is fulfilled, and the kingdom of God has come near; repent, and believe in the good news.”c
1.1–15: Prologue: Jesus's baptism and appearance in Galilee. Mark sets Jesus's story against the backdrop of biblical prophecy and the career of John the Baptist.
1.14–15: Opening proclamation (Mt 4.12–17; Lk 4.14–15).

15 : , i.e., the “season” of God's renewal of the world approaches. Cf. 11.13; 12.2; also Ezek 7.12; Dan 7.22; Rev 1.3. , or “turn around and trust”; Jesus summons exclusive allegiance to God's sovereign power.
As Jesus passed along the Sea of Galilee, he saw Simon and his brother Andrew casting a net into the sea—for they were fishermen.
16 : , technically a freshwater lake, sometimes called Gennesaret. , that is, Peter, Jesus's most prominent disciple (see 3.16; 8.29; 9.2; 14.66–72; 16.7).
And Jesus said to them, “Follow me and I will make you fish for people.”
17 : , both a play on words and a biblical allusion (see Jer 16.16).
And immediately they left their nets and followed him.
As he went a little farther, he saw James son of Zebedee and his brother John, who were in their boat mending the nets.
Immediately he called them; and they left their father Zebedee in the boat with the hired men, and followed him.
1.16–20: First call to discipleship (Mt 4.18–22; cf. Lk 5.1–11; Jn 1.35–51).
20 : , an indication that fishing was a profitable business venture in the first century.
They went to Capernaum; and when the sabbath came, he entered the synagogue and taught.
21 : , located on the sea's northwest coast. , lit., a “gathering,” typically for religious instruction and prayer.
They were astounded at his teaching, for he taught them as one having authority, and not as the scribes.
22 : , religious leaders whose literacy and training in Jewish law gave them elite status and who often oppose Jesus in Mark.
Just then there was in their synagogue a man with an unclean spirit,
23 : , demonic force associated with physical or mental disease; see also 1.27; 3.11,30; 5.2.
and he cried out, “What have you to do with us, Jesus of Nazareth? Have you come to destroy us? I know who you are, the Holy One of God.”
24 : , a name that contrasts with unclean spirit and has both prophetic (2 Kings 4.9) and priestly (Ps 106.16) connotations.
But Jesus rebuked him, saying, “Be silent, and come out of him!”
25 : , a Markan motif that stresses Jesus's authoritative command over evil forces, often to suppress public acclaim; see also 1.34,43–44; 3.12; 8.30; 9.30.
And the unclean spirit, convulsing him and crying with a loud voice, came out of him.
They were all amazed, and they kept on asking one another, “What is this? A new teaching—with authority! He commands even the unclean spirits, and they obey him.”
27 : The exorcism combines with Jesus's teaching (1.22) to confirm his authority in word and deed.
At once his fame began to spread throughout the surrounding region of Galilee.
1.21–28: Jesus in Capernaum (Mt 7.28–29; Lk 4.33–37).
As soon as they left the synagogue, they entered the house of Simon and Andrew, with James and John.
Now Simon's mother-in-law was in bed with a fever, and they told him about her at once.
30 : , suggests that Peter was married. See 1 Cor 9.5.
He came and took her by the hand and lifted her up. Then the fever left her, and she began to serve them.

31 : Jesus's healing power emanates through physical touch, which often violates purity codes that require separation from disease, blood, and death; see also 1.41; 3.10; 5.28; 6.56; 7.33; 8.23. , possibly in a financial sense (cf. 15.41).
That evening, at sunset, they brought to him all who were sick or possessed with demons.
And the whole city was gathered around the door.
And he cured many who were sick with various diseases, and cast out many demons; and he would not permit the demons to speak, because they knew him.
1.29–34: Healings at Simon's house (Mt 8.14–17; Lk 4.38–41).
32–34 : A summary report that is typical In Mark; see also 3.7–12; 6.53–56. , see 1.25n.
In the morning, while it was still very dark, he got up and went out to a deserted place, and there he prayed.
35 : , or “wilderness”; see 1.3n.
And Simon and his companions hunted for him.
When they found him, they said to him, “Everyone is searching for you.”
He answered, “Let us go on to the neighboring towns, so that I may proclaim the message there also; for that is what I came out to do.”
And he went throughout Galilee, proclaiming the message in their synagogues and casting out demons.
1.35–39: Jesus throughout Galilee (Mt 4.23–25; Lk 4.42–44).
39 : Together, Jesus's proclaiming the (lit., “word”) and his bear witness to the dawn of God's kingdom on earth. See 1.27.
A leper came to him begging him, and kneeling he said to him, “If you choose, you can make me clean.”
40 : , a person with a highly contagious skin disease who was deemed ritually impure and thus banished from the community (see Lev 13–14).
Moved with pity, Jesus stretched out his hand and touched him, and said to him, “I do choose. Be made clean!”
41 : , attested in most ancient manuscripts (see also 6.34; 9.22). The alternative reading, which claims Jesus was “moved by anger,” may be original. , see 1.31n.
Immediately the leprosyc left him, and he was made clean.
After sternly warning him he sent him away at once,
saying to him, “See that you say nothing to anyone; but go, show yourself to the priest, and offer for your cleansing what Moses commanded, as a testimony to them.”

43–44 : Earlier, Jesus commanded evil spirits to remain silent (see 1.25n.); here, the injunction applies to the healed man. , Jesus promotes adherence to religious protocol, since it will restore the man's social and religious status.

But he went out and began to proclaim it freely, and to spread the word, so that Jesusf could no longer go into a town openly, but stayed out in the country; and people came to him from every quarter.
1.16–8.26: Jesus's messianic mission: word and deed.
1.16–4.41: Jesus in and around Galilee.
1.40–45: Jesus cures a man who had leprosy (Mt 8.2–4; Lk 5.12–16).
45 : , i.e., the message about God's sovereign power unleashed in the world.
